generated_card.Model details:
### Model details

**Model Architecture:**
*   **Backbone:** MVDream pipeline [RP].
    *   **Stage 1:** Multi-view diffusion network generating four orthogonal and consistent multi-view images from a text prompt and camera embeddings [RP].
    *   **Stage 2:** Multi-view Score Distillation Sampling (MV-SDS) to produce a detailed 3D NeRF model [RP].
*   **Key Mechanism:** Diffusion of multi-view images using a global aligned camera coordination system [RP].
    *   The image from a default camera (no azimuth rotation) is consistently the front view of the object [RP].
    *   Alignment is achieved by matching the CLIP image feature of a view to the "front view" CLIP text feature embeddings [RP].
*   **ImageDream Adaptation:**
    *   Adds a new set of MLPs ($\theta k_g,l$ and $\theta v_g,l$) within attention layers for image cross-attention [RP].
    *   Introduces a weight $\lambda = 1.0$ to balance hidden features from text and image [RP].
    *   Employs a **Pixel Controller** by embedding the image prompt pixel latent across all attention layers [RP].
    *   Modifies MVDream's 3D dense self-attention from (bz, 4, c, h_l, w_l) to (bz, 5, c, h_l, w_l) by concatenating the input image [RP].
    *   **Camera Alignment:** Narrows the parameter sampling range for camera FoV and elevation from MVDream's [15, 60], [0, 30] to [45, 50] and [0, 5], respectively [RP].
*   **Input:** An image of an object rendered from a random viewpoint with a random camera ($x_r$) [RP].
*   **Output:** Canonical multi-view images of the object as depicted in the input image [RP]. The MV dataset is formally represented as Xmv = {xmv, y, xr, cmv}, where cmv are canonical cameras [RP].

**Intended uses:**
*   Generating 3D NeRF models from an input image prompt, aiming for superior geometric accuracy compared to systems using relative camera coordination [RP].
*   The model can absorb information like object structure from the input image [RP].

**Additional information:**
*   **Model Version:** No info available.
*   **Developer(s):** No info available.
*   **Release Date:** Following submission [RP].
*   **License Type:** No info available.
*   **Reference Paper:** MVDream [37] [RP].
*   **Technical Report:** No info available.
*   **Citation:** No info available.
*   **Acknowledgements:**
    *   This codebase is modified from $10:LINK$ mvdream-hf [HF].
    *   The original papers: MVDream.
*   **Disclaimer:**
    *   The pixel controller model struggles to capture small facial details in full-body avatars when image constraints are overly stringent [RP].
    *   Future work includes exploring larger models like SDXL [29] to address limitations [RP].
    *   Converted fp16 weights are provided on HuggingFace: https://huggingface.co/Peng-Wang/ImageDream MVDream https://github.com/ashawkey/mvdream_diffusers ImageDream [HF].

generated_card.Training:
### Training

**Dataset:**
*   **Data collection process:**
    *   Joint training on rendered datasets from Objaverse [RP].
    *   Larger-scale text-to-image (t2i) dataset: LAION5B [RP].
    *   Both 3D rendered datasets and 2D image datasets were used for controller training [RP].
    *   For image prompts in the 3D dataset, one of 16 front-side views (azimuth angles $\left[-90, 90\right]$ degrees) was randomly selected from 32 circle views [RP].
    *   For the 2D dataset, the input image was used as the image prompt [RP].
*   **Number of samples:** No info available.

**Pre-processing:**
*   **Data preparation:**
    *   Input image prompt latents were not noised during diffusion network training [RP].
    *   An all-zero vector was assigned to the camera embedding of the input image to differentiate input image features [RP].
    *   A random dropout rate of 0.1 was set for the image prompt during training; dropped prompts were replaced with a random uni-colored image [RP].
    *   Test image prompts were resized to 256x256 [RP].
    *   For NeRF optimization, the image-prompt background was adjusted to match the rendered background color from NeRF to prevent floating artifacts [RP].

**Hyperparameter details:**
*   **Learning rate:**
    *   $1e-4$ for global and local controllers [RP].
    *   $1e-5$ for the model with the pixel controller [RP].
    *   Learning rate for pixel controller was reduced by a factor of ten compared to global/local controllers [RP].
*   **Batch size:** 256 [RP].
*   **Optimizer type:** AdamW [RP].
*   **Epoch count:** No info available.
*   **Loss function:**
    *   Multi-view diffusion (MV) diffusion loss (Equation 1: <math display="block">\mathcal{L}_{\text{MV}}(\theta, \mathcal{X}, \mathcal{X}_{mv}) = \mathbb{E}_{\boldsymbol{x}, y, \mathbf{c}, t, \epsilon} \left[ \| \epsilon - \epsilon_{\theta}(\boldsymbol{x}^{p}; y, \boldsymbol{c}^{p}, t) \|_{2}^{2} \right] </math>) [RP].
    *   Score-distillation sampling (SDS) based on four generated views for DreamFusion pipeline (Equation 2: <math display="block">\mathcal{L}_{\text{MV-SDS}}(\phi, \boldsymbol{x}^{mv}) = \mathbb{E}_{t, \mathbf{c}, \epsilon} \left[ \| \boldsymbol{x}^{mv} - \hat{\boldsymbol{x}}_0^{mv} \|_2^2 \right]. </math>) [RP].
*   **Gradient accumulation:** 2 [RP].
*   **Diffusion CFG (class free guidance):** 5.0 for testing [RP].

**Training:**
*   **Training procedures:**
    *   **Initial training (MVDream):** Each block of the multi-view network uses 3D attention on four view images for correspondence learning [RP].
    *   **Controller training:**
        *   The diffusion model is frozen [RP].
        *   Only controller parameters ($\theta_g$, $\theta_{kg,l}$, $\theta_{vg,l}$, $\theta_h$, $\theta_{kh,l}$, $\theta_{vh,l}$, $\theta_r$, $\theta_{k r,l}$, $\theta_{v r,l}$) are fine-tuned [RP].
        *   Pixel controller integrated into multi-view diffusion without extra parameters; all feature parameters fine-tuned in unison [RP].
    *   **NeRF optimization:**
        *   Follows MVDream's configuration but with a three-stage optimization [RP].
        *   Resolutions: [64, 192, 256] switched at [5K, 10K] steps [RP].
        *   Camera distance between [0.6, 0.85] [RP].
*   **Training setup:**
    *   **Hardware:** 8 A100 GPUs for controller training [RP], A100 for NeRF optimization [RP].
    *   **Duration:**
        *   About 2 days for controller training [RP].
        *   About 1 hour for NeRF optimization [RP].
    *   **Resources:** Initialized from Stable Diffusion (2.1) checkpoint of MVDream [RP].
    *   **Steps:** 60K steps for controller training [RP].

generated_card.Evaluation:
### Evaluation

*   **Evaluation setup**:
    *   **Metrics**:
        *   **Qualitative Evaluation**: User study based on **geometry quality** and **similarity to image prompt (IP)** [RP].
            *   **Geometry quality** assessed conformance to common sense shape and minimal artifacts [RP].
            *   **Similarity to IP** assessed resemblance to the input image [RP].
        *   **Numerical Evaluation**:
            *   Modified **Inception Score (QIS)**: Evaluates image quality, diversity component omitted [RP].
                *   Calculated as `<math display="inline">\sum_i p_i \log(Np_i)</math>`, where N is inception class count and `<math display="inline">p_i</math>` is predicted probability for `<math display="inline">i_{th}</math>` class [RP].
            *   **CLIP Score**: Assesses text-image and image-image alignment [RP].
                *   Calculated as the mean score between each generated view and the provided prompt [RP].
    *   **Test Sets/Data**: User study lacked ground truth for test image prompts [RP].
    *   **Procedures**:
        *   **Qualitative**: Double-blind user study where participants chose preferred 3D assets without identifying labels [RP].
        *   **Quantitative**: Baselines tested using default configurations in threestudio 1 [RP].

*   **Evaluation Results**:
    *   **Qualitative**:
        *   **ImageDream** (with and without pixel controller) significantly outperformed baselines [RP].
        *   **ImageDream-P** (with pixel controller) was particularly favored [RP].
        *   **ImageDream-G** (without pixel controller) received a positive preference rate [RP].
        *   **SyncDreamer** results for NeuS had a **0% preference rate** and were omitted [RP].
        *   **Magic123** and **Zero123** often produced incorrect geometry, e.g., inability to accurately represent a horse body [RP].
    *   **Numerical (Table 1)**:
        *   **SD-XL** achieved the highest **QIS** and **CLIP scores** (reflecting scores of test images) [RP].
        *   **ImageDream-pixel** ranked second in other scores, despite **Zero123-XL** using a significantly larger dataset (Objaverse-XL) [RP].
        *   **MVDream** showed improved synthesized image quality after 3D fusion due to multi-view consistency [RP].
        *   **Zero123** and **Zero123-XL** experienced a drop in image quality post-3D fusion due to diffusion inconsistency [RP].
        *   **Magic123** enhanced **CLIP score** over **Zero123** by integrating a joint diffusion model [RP].
        *   **SyncDreamer's** quality declined due to diffusing only 16 fixed views, complicating reconstruction [RP].
        *   **ImageDream** maintained high image quality in both diffusion and post-3D fusion stages [RP].
        *   **ImageDream's local controller** provided better image **CLIP scores** post-fusion due to richer image feature representations [RP].
        *   **ImageDream's pixel controller model** excelled in image **CLIP scores** during both diffusion and post-3D fusion stages [RP].

*   **Performance Comparison**:
    *   **ImageDream** was compared against **Zero123-XL** (trained on 10x larger data), **Magic123**, and **SyncDreamer** [RP].
    *   **ImageDream** resolves issues with incorrect geometry (e.g., in Zero123 and Magic123) through its design, leading to more satisfactory models [RP].
    *   Numerical scores (QIS, CLIP) do not fully capture crucial aspects like **multi-view consistency** and **geometric correctness** [RP].
    *   **Zero123-XL**, despite high IS, showed poorer consistency [RP].
    *   While scores offer reliability when consistency is high, more comprehensive metrics for geometric correctness are needed for future comparisons [RP].

generated_card.Model details:
### Model details

**Model Architecture**:
*   ControlNet is a neural network architecture designed to add **spatial and task-specific conditions** to large pre-trained text-to-image diffusion models [RP].
*   It operates by injecting conditions into network "blocks" [RP].
*   ControlNet creates a **trainable copy** of neural network blocks, distinct from a "locked" copy that retains original weights [GH].
*   The architecture reuses a deep encoder, specifically the **Stable Diffusion encoder**, as a backbone for learning diverse controls [GH].
*   When applied to Stable Diffusion, ControlNet targets the **U-Net** structure, which includes an encoder, a middle block, and a skip-connected decoder [RP].
    *   The Stable Diffusion U-Net consists of 25 blocks: 12 encoder blocks, 1 middle block, and 12 decoder blocks [RP].
    *   Eight blocks are for down-sampling or up-sampling convolution layers, and 17 blocks are main blocks [RP].
    *   Each main block contains **4 ResNet layers** and **2 Vision Transformers (ViTs)** [RP].
*   ControlNet creates a trainable copy of the **12 encoding blocks and 1 middle block** of Stable Diffusion [RP].
*   These 12 encoding blocks are across **4 resolutions** (64x64, 32x32, 16x16, 8x8), with each repeated three times [RP].
*   The outputs of ControlNet are added to the **12 skip-connections and 1 middle block** of the U-Net [RP].
*   **Input**:
    *   **Text prompts** are encoded using the **CLIP text encoder** [RP].
    *   **Diffusion timesteps** are encoded with a **time encoder** using positional encoding [RP].
    *   **Conditioning images** are added to guide image generation [RP].
    *   The ControlNet for depth maps can receive **512x512 depth maps**, differing from Stability's SD2 depth model that uses 64x64 depth maps [GH].
*   **Output**: High-quality generated images based on the input text and conditioning [RP].

**Intended uses**:
*   To enable **conditional control** over large pre-trained text-to-image diffusion models [GH].
*   To add **extra conditions** to diffusion models, such as Canny edges, depth maps, M-LSD lines, HED boundaries, user scribbles, human pose, semantic segmentation, and normal maps [HF, GH].
*   For **generating images** with spatially localized, task-specific image conditions [RP].
*   Particularly recommended for use with **Stable Diffusion v1.5** [HF].
*   Can be used with other diffusion models, including **DreamBooth-trained Stable Diffusion models** [HF].
*   Supports **composing multiple ControlNets** to apply multiple conditioning images (e.g., Canny edges and pose) to a single Stable Diffusion instance [RP, GH].
*   Allows for **transferring to community models** without changing the network topology of pre-trained SD models [RP, GH].
*   Features a **"guess mode" (non-prompt mode)** where the ControlNet encoder interprets the content of the input control map even without text prompts [GH].

**Additional information**:
*   **Model Version(s)**: ControlNet 1.0 [GH]. ControlNet 1.1 has been released as a nightly version and will be merged into the repository [GH].
*   **Developer(s)**: Lvmin Zhang, Maneesh Agrawala [HF].
*   **Release Date**: No specific release date for 1.0 provided. News about ControlNet 1.1 was updated on 2023/04/14 [GH].
*   **License Type**: No info available.
*   **Reference Paper**: "Adding Conditional Control to Text-to-Image Diffusion Models" by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala [HF, GH].
*   **Citation**: No specific citation format provided, but the paper named above is the primary reference [HF, GH].
*   **Acknowledgements**:
    *   Hysts for making the Hugging Face Space and Colab list [GH].
    *   haofanwang for ControlNet-for-Diffusers [GH].
    *   Various authors for making ControlNet demos, including fffiloni, other-model, ThereforeGames, RamAnanth1 [GH].
*   **Disclaimer**: Ablation studies indicate that using a deep encoder is crucial for ControlNet's performance, contrasting with lighter models or MLPs [GH].

generated_card.Training:
### Training
**Dataset:**
*   **Data Collection Process:**
    *   ControlNet learns task-specific conditions in an end-to-end manner [RP].
    *   Can use small datasets (< 50k samples) without collapsing [RP].
    *   One ControlNet for Stable Diffusion V2 Depth-to-Image (SDv2-D2I) was trained with 200k training samples [RP].
    *   The depth model was trained on 3M depth-image, caption pairs [RP]. Depth images were generated with Midas [RP].
*   **Number of Samples:**
    *   Robust with limited 1k images [RP].
    *   Scalable to millions to billions of data if powerful computation clusters are available [RP].
    *   Depth ControlNet trained with 200k samples for SD V2 [RP].
    *   Depth model for SD 1.5 trained on 3M depth-image, caption pairs [RP].

**Pre-processing:**
*   **Data Preparation:**
    *   Stable Diffusion uses latent images (64x64) encoded from 512x512 pixel-space images [RP].
    *   Input conditioning images (e.g., edge, pose, depth) are converted from 512x512 to a 64x64 feature space vector [RP].
    *   A tiny network E(·) of four convolution layers encodes the image-space condition into a feature space conditioning vector <math display="block">c_{\rm f} = \mathcal{E}(c_{\rm i}).</math> [RP].
    *   This encoding network uses 4x4 kernels, 2x2 strides, and ReLU activations [RP]. It uses 16, 32, 64, 128 channels and is initialized with Gaussian weights, trained jointly with the full model [RP].
*   **Data Augmentation:**
    *   During training, 50% of text prompts are randomly replaced with empty strings [RP]. This increases ControlNet's ability to recognize semantics directly from input conditioning images [RP].

**Hyperparameter details:**
*   **Learning Objective:** The diffusion model's overall learning objective <math display="block">\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{\boldsymbol{z}_0, \boldsymbol{t}, \boldsymbol{c}_t, \boldsymbol{c}_t, \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_c, \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)} \Big[ \| \epsilon - \epsilon_{\theta}(\boldsymbol{z}_t, \boldsymbol{t}, \boldsymbol{c}_t, \boldsymbol{c}_t)) \|_2^2 \Big], \quad (5)</math> is directly used in fine-tuning ControlNet [RP].
*   **Optimization Steps:** Model abruptly succeeds in following input conditions, usually in less than **10K optimization steps**. This is called "sudden convergence" [RP].

**Training:**
*   **Training Procedures:**
    *   Parameters (Θ) of the original pre-trained neural block are locked (frozen) [RP].
    *   The original block is cloned into a **trainable copy** with parameters Θc [RP].
    *   The trainable copy takes an external conditioning vector `c` as input [RP].
    *   The trainable copy is connected to the locked model with **zero convolution** layers, Z(·; ·) [RP].
    *   Zero convolutions are 1x1 convolution layers with both weight and bias initialized to zeros [RP].
    *   Two instances of zero convolutions with parameters Θz1 and Θz2 are used to build a ControlNet [RP].
    *   In the first training step, zero convolutions output zeros, preventing harmful noise from influencing hidden states [RP].
    *   This preserves the large-scale pre-trained model as a backbone, allowing for robust handling of diverse conditions [RP].
    *   No layer is trained from scratch; it is a fine-tuning process [RP].
    *   Replacing zero convolutions with standard ones destroys the pre-trained backbone, significantly reducing performance [RP].
*   **Training Setup:**
    *   Training is computationally efficient due to frozen locked copy parameters, which require no gradient computation [RP].
    *   Speeds up training and saves GPU memory [RP].
    *   Optimizing Stable Diffusion with ControlNet requires about 23% more GPU memory and 34% more time per iteration compared to optimizing without ControlNet (on NVIDIA A100 PCIE 40GB) [RP].
    *   Can be trained on personal devices [RP].
    *   A ControlNet for SD V2 was trained on a single NVIDIA RTX 3090Ti [RP].
    *   The depth model for SD 1.5 was trained using Nvidia A100 80G [RP].
*   **Duration:**
    *   The SD V2 ControlNet (depth) was trained in 5 days [RP].
    *   The depth model for SD 1.5 was trained for 500 GPU-hours [RP].
*   **Resources:**
    *   One single NVIDIA RTX 3090Ti was used for SD V2 ControlNet [RP].
    *   Nvidia A100 80G was used for the depth model [RP].
    *   Low VRAM mode is available for 8GB GPUs or larger batch sizes [RP].
*   **Checkpoints:**
    *   8 different checkpoints were released, each trained with Stable Diffusion v1-5 for a specific conditioning type [RP].

generated_card.Evaluation:
### Evaluation
*   **Evaluation Setup**:
    *   **Metrics**:
        *   **User Study**:
            *   **Average Human Ranking (AHR)** for "quality of displayed images" and "fidelity to the sketch" [RP].
            *   Scale: 1 to 5, where lower is worse [RP].
        *   **Condition Reconstruction**:
            *   **Intersection-over-Union (IoU)**: Measures segmentation fidelity [RP].
            *   Calculated by re-detecting segmentations using OneFormer [35] [RP].
        *   **Image Quality/Distribution**:
            *   **Frechet Inception Distance (FID)** [28]: Measures distribution distance between generated and real image sets [RP].
            *   **CLIP scores** [66]: Text-image similarity [RP].
            *   **CLIP aesthetic score** [79] [RP].
    *   **Test Sets/Data**:
        *   **User Study**: 20 unseen hand-drawn sketches [RP].
        *   **Condition Reconstruction/FID**: Test set of **ADE20K** [96] [RP].
    *   **Procedures**:
        *   **Quantitative Analysis**:
            *   **User Study**: 12 users ranked 20 groups of 5 results [RP]. Each group contained output from PITI [89], Sketch-Guided Diffusion (SGD) [88] (default edge-guidance <math display="inline">\beta=1.6</math> and high edge-guidance <math display="inline">\beta=3.2</math>), ControlNet-lite, and ControlNet [RP]. This yielded 100 rankings for quality and 100 for fidelity [RP].
            *   **Condition Reconstruction**: Generated images with ADE20K segmentations, then applied OneFormer [35] to detect segmentations for IoU calculation [RP].
            *   **FID**: Measured over randomly generated <math display="inline">512 \times 512</math> image sets using different segmentation-conditioned methods [RP].
        *   **Qualitative Analysis**: Visual comparison of baselines (PITI [89], Sketch-Guided Diffusion [88], Taming Transformers [19]) against ControlNet [RP].

*   **Evaluation Results**:
    *   **User Study (AHR)**: "Average rankings are shown in Table 1" [RP] (specific values not provided in text).
    *   **Condition Reconstruction (IoU)**: "reconstructed IoUs (Table 2)" [RP] (specific values not provided in text). State-of-the-art segmentation method OneFormer [35] achieves an IoU of **0.58** on the ground-truth set [RP].
    *   **FID, CLIP, CLIP Aesthetic Scores**: "Table 3" [RP] (specific values not provided in text).

*   **Performance Comparison**:
    *   ControlNet robustly handles diverse conditioning images [RP].
    *   Achieves **sharp and clean results** [RP].
    *   Visual comparisons show ControlNet against PITI [89], Sketch-Guided Diffusion [88], and Taming Transformers [19] [RP].
    *   PITI's backbone is OpenAI GLIDE [57], which has different visual quality and performance [RP].
    *   No explicit statement on achieving state-of-the-art results (SOTA) or superior performance to all comparable models, but results are visually compelling [RP].

generated_card.Model details:
### Model details

**Model Architecture**
*   **Backbone**: Image-encoder-text-decoder based on **ViT** (Vision Transformer) [RP].
*   **Input Representation**:
    *   **Image**: Utilizes a variable-resolution input representation to handle diverse aspect ratios and resolutions [RP].
        *   Scales input images to extract the maximal number of fixed-size patches that fit within a given sequence length [RP].
        *   Employs **2-dimensional absolute positional embeddings** for input patches to manage variable resolutions [RP].
    *   **Text/Prompts**: Language prompts (e.g., questions) are rendered directly onto the input image [RP]. This allows Pix2Struct to read questions and images jointly via the visual modality [RP].
*   **Output Format**:
    *   **HTML DOM Tree**: During pretraining, learns to parse masked screenshots of web pages into a simplified HTML [RP].
        *   Condenses the HTML DOM tree by keeping nodes with visible elements or descendants [RP].
        *   Replaces single-child nodes with grandchildren to remove chained nesting [RP].
        *   Uses only text, filenames, and alt-text of images from nodes [RP].
    *   **Text**: For tasks like captioning or VQA, the output is text [RP].
        *   The **decoder sequence length** is reduced by finding the largest linearized subtree fitting within a predefined sequence length [RP].
*   **Pretraining Objective**: Uses a **BART-like** learning signal by masking 50% of the text and decoding the entire subtree [RP].

**Intended uses**
*   **Primary Use Case**: A pretrained **image-to-text model** for purely **visual language understanding** [RP].
*   **Finetuning**: Can be finetuned on tasks involving visually-situated language across various domains [RP].
*   **Supported Tasks**:
    *   **Image Captioning**: Applies to tasks like Screen2Words and Widget Captioning [RP].
        *   For Widget Captioning, inputs include an image with a bounding box around a specific widget [RP].
    *   **Visual Question Answering (VQA)**: Used for tasks like OCR-VQA, ChartQA, DocVQA, and InfographicsVQA [RP].
        *   Questions are rendered as a header at the top of the original image [RP].
        *   For multiple-choice answers, choices are also rendered in the header [RP].
    *   **Referring Expression Comprehension (RefExp)**: The task is to identify a UI component referred to by a natural language expression [RP].
        *   Input: An image with a bounding box around a candidate object and a header with the referring expression [HF].
        *   Output: The model predicts "true" or "false" for each candidate [RP].

**Additional information**
*   **Developer(s)**: No info available.
*   **Release Date**: No info available.
*   **Model Version(s)**: **Base** and **Large** models are available as pretrained checkpoints [RP].
*   **License**: No info available.
*   **Reference Paper**: If you want to cite this work, please consider citing the original paper: [PP_16:CODE] [RP, HF].
*   **Citation**:
    ```
    @article{pix2struct,
      title={Pix2Struct: Screenshot Parsing as Pretraining for Visual Language Understanding},
      author={xyz},
      journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:2207.07106},
      year={2022}
    }
    ```
    (Note: The full citation from the reference text was not provided, but the paper title and year are mentioned in passing at the end of the input.)
*   **Acknowledgements**: No info available.
*   **Disclaimer**: Future versions of general-purpose visual language understanding models will benefit from better data curation [RP]. Care must be taken regarding harmful content on the web, to which multimodal models would also be sensitive [RP].

generated_card.Training:
### Training

**Dataset:**
*   **Pretraining Data:**
    *   Constructed from **URLs in the C4 corpus** [RP].
    *   **80M pairs** of screenshots and their corresponding HTML source [RP].
    *   Screenshots have a width of **1024 pixels**, with height fit to content [RP].
*   **Warmup Stage Data:**
    *   Images of text snippets created from **BooksCorpus** [RP].
    *   Text snippets are up to **128 bytes long** [RP].
    *   Image width is **640 pixels**, height fits content [RP].
    *   Text is **unmasked** [RP].
*   **Finetuning Data:**
    *   Requires preprocessing to match task-specific image inputs and text outputs [RP].
    *   Example: OCR-VQA data downloaded from their website [RP].

**Pre-processing:**
*   Input images are rescaled to **prevent aspect ratio distortion** [RP].
    *   Rescaling extracts the maximal number of patches within a given sequence length [RP].
    *   This differs from methods that rescale to fixed resolutions (which can distort aspect ratios) [RP].
*   Screenshot and HTML are **modified for rich and dense learning signal** during pretraining [RP].
    *   This balances semantic preservation and practical decoder sequence length [RP].
*   Data preprocessing scripts use **Apache Beam library via Dataflow**, or can be run locally [RP].

**Hyperparameter details:**
*   **Optimizer:** Adafactor [RP].
*   **Learning Rate Schedule:** Linear warmup of **1000 steps** to **0.01**, then cosine decay to **0** [RP].

**Training:**
*   **Procedure:**
    *   **Warmup Stage:**
        *   Initial "learning to read" stage (curricular learning) [RP].
        *   Trained to decode original text from text snippet images [RP].
        *   Improves pretraining stability, increases convergence speed, and enhances finetuning performance [RP].
        *   Duration: **30K steps** for both base and large models [RP].
        *   Maximum input sequence length: **128 patches** [RP].
    *   **Pretraining (Screenshot Parsing Objective):**
        *   Goal: Representing the underlying structure of the input image [RP].
        *   Uses self-supervised pairs of input images and target text from web pages [RP].
        *   Integrates signals akin to OCR, masked language modeling, and alt-text recovery [RP].
        *   **Base Model:**
            *   Further pretrained for **270K steps** after warmup [RP].
            *   Batch size: **2048** [RP].
        *   **Large Model:**
            *   Pretrained for **170K steps** after warmup [RP].
            *   Batch size: **1024** [RP].
        *   Input sequence length: **2048 patches** [RP].
        *   Decoder sequence length: **128 tokens** [RP].
        *   Pretraining targets: Maximum **1024 characters** [RP].
    *   **Finetuning:**
        *   Follows pretraining [RP].
        *   Input sequence length: **4096** for base, **3072** for large [RP].
        *   Base model on InfographicVQA uses **6144** [RP].
        *   Duration: **5000 or 10000 steps** [RP].
        *   Batch size: **32, 128, or 256** [RP].
        *   Includes hyperparameter tuning and early stopping based on validation set [RP].
*   **Training Setup:**
    *   **Base Model:** Trained on **64 Google Cloud TPUs** [RP].
    *   **Large Model:** Trained on **128 Google Cloud TPUs** [RP].
    *   Experimental implementation uses a wrapper around the **T5X library** [RP].
    *   Training loop consists of interleaved train steps and evaluations [RP].

generated_card.Evaluation:
### Evaluation

- **Evaluation Setup**:
    - **Domains**: Illustrations, User Interfaces, Natural Images, Documents [RP].
    - **Evaluation Data**: Standard splits of aggregated datasets, optimized for diversity [RP].
        - Datasets are summarized in Table 4 (Appendix C) [RP].
        - No additional labeled data was used [RP].
    - **Metrics**:
        - **Average Normalized Levenshtein Similarity (ANLS)**: DocVQA, InfographicVQA [RP].
        - **Exact Match (EM)**: AI2D, RefExp, OCR-VQA [RP].
        - **Relaxed Accuracy (RA)**: ChartQA [RP].
        - **CIDEr**: Generation tasks [RP].
    - **Procedure**: Model evaluated on test set using provided command [RP].
        - Requires replacing checkpoint path with validation-selected checkpoint [RP].
        - Config file under `configs/sizes` must be consistent with checkpoint [RP].

- **Evaluation Results**:
    - **InfographicVQA**:
        - Pix2Struct-Large achieved **ANLS of 40**, setting State-of-the-Art (SotA) among visual models [RP].
        - Donut had an **ANLS of 11.6**, potentially due to scaling extreme aspect ratio images [RP].
        - T5-based (text-only) model achieved **ANLS of 46.1** [RP].
    - **DocVQA**:
        - T5-based (text-only) model achieved **ANLS of 81** [RP].
        - Visual context plays a lesser role in text-heavy datasets like DocVQA [RP].
    - **General Performance**:
        - Full results for pipeline and pixel-only methods are in Table 3 [RP].
        - Checkpoints are compatible with evaluation and inference commands [RP].

- **Performance Comparison**:
    - Pix2Struct was compared against **State-of-the-Art (SotA)** methods in each domain [RP].
    - **Comparison Focus**: Single-model, single-task baselines trained on standard splits for fair comparison [RP].
    - **Baseline Limitations**:
        - Some SotA models use ensembles, multitask training with external data, or train with validation data [RP].
        - Several (per-task) SotA methods use **domain-specific inputs** (e.g., view hierarchies for UIs, gold data tables for charts), making cross-domain application difficult [RP].
    - **Consistent Visual Baseline**: Donut was finetuned for tasks lacking a purely visual baseline [RP].
    - **SotA Achievement**: Pix2Struct-Large sets the SotA among visual models for InfographicVQA with an ANLS of 40 [RP].
    - **Text-only Model Performance**: Text-only baselines (e.g., T5-based) achieve near or exactly SotA results on DocVQA and InfographicVQA due to the text-heavy nature of the data and mature text encoders [RP].

generated_card.Model details:
### Model details

**Model Architecture:**
*   Models dynamics using a low-rank approximation of particle deformations over time [RP].
*   Deformation matrix $\mathrm{D} \in \mathbb{R}^{dN \times T}$ is approximated as $\mathrm{D} \approx \mathrm{WV}$, where $\mathrm{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{dN \times K}$ is the spatial weight matrix and $\mathrm{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times T}$ is the temporal basis matrix [RP].
*   **N**: Number of particles (vertices for meshes, splats for 3D Gaussians) [RP].
*   **T**: Number of timestamps in the dynamic sequence [RP].
*   **d**: Degrees of freedom (e.g., d=6 for rigid transformations) [RP].
*   **K**: Desired rank of the approximation [RP].
*   Utilizes a particle-based shape autoencoder to describe input geometry as compact neural latent representations [RP].
*   Geometry-aware attention module incorporates per-point normal values from input meshes to address blend weight corruption [RP].
*   Employs spatially continuous decoding for blend weights using an MLP-based head [RP].
*   Uses learnable semantic queries for discrete per-bone attributes (joint positions and pose-to-rest transformations) [RP].
*   Detects bone attributes via two individual bone attribute decoders [RP].
*   Pose decoder outputs 8D dual quaternions for rigid transformations [RP].
*   Includes a structure-aware transformer with a novel "next-child-bone prediction" architecture for improved bone attribute prediction [RP].
*   Causal attention layers enhance bone-wise embeddings by learning from ancestral bones in the kinematic tree [RP].

**Intended uses:**
*   Primary use case: Authoring animation-ready 3D characters from various 3D shapes [RP].
*   Generates animation assets: K joints (bone head and tail positions), 6-DoF transformations to a common rest pose, and blend weights between joints and particles [RP].
*   Supports fine-grained control of fingers and adaptively changes bone length for unusual shapes [RP].
*   Handles complex input poses (far from T-/A-pose) and transforms them to T-pose for animation [RP].
*   Efficient for high-polygon models, processing over 1 million triangular faces in under 3 seconds [RP].
*   Supports asymmetric inputs and extends to extra bones (e.g., tails, ears) with fine-tuning [RP].
*   Provides a template-free pipeline, applicable to a wider range of character shapes than SMPL-based methods [RP].

**Additional information:**
*   **Model Name**: Make-It-Animatable: An Efficient Framework for Authoring Animation-Ready 3D Characters [RP].
*   **Developers**: Zhiyang Guo, Jinxu Xiang, Kai Ma, Wengang Zhou, Houqiang Li, Ran Zhang [RP].
*   **Release Date**: No info available.
*   **License Type**: No info available.
*   **Reference Paper**: "Make-It-Animatable: An Efficient Framework for Authoring Animation-Ready 3D Characters (CVPR 2025 Highlight)" [RP].
*   **Project Page**: [RP]
*   **Code**: [RP]
*   **Pretrained Models**: [PP_6:CODE] [RP]
*   **Acknowledgements**: $19:LINK$ 1zb/3DShape2VecSet, $20:LINK$ Adobe Mixamo, $21:LINK$ 3DGS Render Blender Addon by KIRI Engine [RP].
*   **Disclaimer**: Hope this can be resolved by future updates of Blender [RP].

generated_card.Training:
### Training

**Dataset:**
*   **Data collection process:**
    *   Utilized artist-designed 3D models from Mixamo [1] [RP].
    *   Comprises 95 high-quality characters and 2,453 diverse motion sequences [RP].
    *   Each character is preprocessed to conform to a standard skeleton structure with K = 52 bones [RP].
    *   Motion sequences contain an average of 200 frames [RP].
    *   An additional 35 distinct anime characters (30 for training, 5 for validation) were manually created using VRoid Studio [45] [RP]. These include accessories like animal ears and tails [RP].
    *   These additional characters were preprocessed to match the Mixamo skeleton definition, with extra bones for accessories [RP].
    *   Some samples from the 3DBiCar Dataset, rigged by Mixamo, were included to improve generalization to characters with unusual head-body ratios [GH].
    *   During training, online pairing of characters and animations is performed using Blender to reduce storage and ensure diversity [GH]. This involves sampling character-motion pairs, applying motion, and optionally retargeting [GH].
    *   Training samples are generated by extracting ground-truth data (blend weights, bone positions, poses) and sampling points from the animated mesh surface [GH].
*   **Number of samples:**
    *   95% of data used for training, 5% for validation [RP].
    *   Effective dataset size equivalent to over 40 million frames due to random character-motion pair selection during each iteration [RP].

**Pre-processing:**
*   **Data preparation:**
    *   Input character shapes are re-centered and normalized [RP].
    *   Characters are preprocessed to a standard skeleton structure with 52 bones [RP].
    *   Non-standard bone blend weights are transferred to topologically nearest ancestral bones within the standard skeleton [RP].
    *   Missing standard bones are masked during loss computation [RP].
    *   For fine-stage training, input characters are transformed to canonical coordinates [RP].
    *   Canonical transformation: aligns shape to a common orientation to resolve pose ambiguity [RP].
        *   Hip (root), and two upper thighs define a "hip plane" [RP].
        *   Hip is located at the origin [RP].
        *   Normal of the hip plane is aligned with the z-axis [RP].
        *   Vector from right to left thigh is parallel to the x-axis [RP].
*   **Preprocessing methods:**
    *   Point cloud sampling: input N = 32768 points are sampled from the surface of the input mesh [RP].
    *   Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) [47]: used to downsample the point cloud to M points [RP].
    *   Hierarchical sampling: For fine training or inference, 50% of points are sampled uniformly, and 50% are sampled near hand joints [RP]. This ensures higher sampling density in key regions like hands [RP].
    *   Positional encoding [RP].

**Hyperparameter details:**
*   **Learning rate:** Linearly increased to 1e-4 within the first 1% iterations (warm-up), then gradually decreased using cosine decay to 1e-5 [RP].
*   **Batch size:** Value present in `train.sh` script, can be decreased to reduce VRAM usage [GH]. No specific value provided in prose.
*   **Optimizer type:** No info available.
*   **Epoch count:** No info available.
*   **Loss function:** Supervised by L losses with ground-truth blend weights, bone positions, pose-to-rest transformations, and extra body prior losses [RP].
*   **Shape autoencoder hyperparameters:** Consistent with 3DShape2VecSet [58], using shape latents F ∈ RM×C with M = 512 and C = 512 [RP].
*   **Shape latent initialization:** By applying Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) on the input point cloud [RP].
*   **Pretrained weights:** Optionally utilize pretrained weights for the autoencoder (for training from scratch) 

{'type': 'block_code', 'raw': 'git -C /tmp/hf-data lfs pull -I output/ae\ncp -r /tmp/hf-data/output/ae output/\n', 'style': 'fenced', 'marker': '```', 'attrs': {'info': 'bash'}}

 [GH].

**Training:**
*   **Training procedures:**
    *   End-to-end data-driven training [RP].
    *   Coarse-to-fine training strategy adopted [RP].
    *   **Coarse stage:**
        *   Uniformly samples the input shape [RP].
        *   Predicts only bone positions [RP].
        *   Applies random 3D rotations for data augmentation [RP].
        *   Provides rough localization information [RP].
    *   **Fine stage:**
        *   Transforms input character to canonical coordinates [RP].
        *   Applies hierarchical sampling [RP].
    *   **Shape encoder:** Parameters are frozen during training as it's pretrained on ShapeNet [11] [RP].
    *   **Additional skeleton topologies:** Fine-tuning the final layer of the weight decoder and extra learnable queries to adapt [RP].
    *   Models for predicting weights, bones, and poses are trained separately by default, but can be trained jointly by enabling `--predict_*` flags [GH].
*   **Training setup:**
    *   **Hardware:** Trained on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs [RP].
    *   **Duration:** No info available.
    *   **Resources:** Hyperparameters in `train.sh` are set for A100 80G GPU [GH]. VRAM usage can be reduced by decreasing batch size and `sample_frames` [GH].
*   **Generalization improvements:**
    *   Dual-quaternion representation for pose-to-rest transformations is replaced by `ortho6d` along with joint positions [GH].
    *   Inclusion of samples from the 3DBiCar Dataset [GH].

generated_card.Evaluation:
### Evaluation and Benchmarking

*   **Evaluation Setup**:
    *   **Quantitative Evaluation**:
        *   **Test Set**: Bipedal humanoid characters from 'ModelsResource-RigNetv1' dataset [56].
        *   **Procedure**: Comparison with existing automatic rigging algorithms [56].
        *   **Metrics**:
            *   Percentage error between predicted and ground-truth animation assets (for Mixamo test split ablations) [RP].
            *   **IoU**: Intersection over Union of bone matching [56].
            *   **Precision**: Bone matching precision [56].
            *   **Recall**: Bone matching recall [56].
            *   **CD-J2J**: Chamfer distance between joints [56].
            *   **CD-J2B**: Chamfer distance between joints and bone line segments [56].
            *   **CD-B2B**: Chamfer distance between bone line segments [56].
    *   **Qualitative Evaluation**:
        *   **Data Sources**: Diverse in-the-wild 3D characters from Objaverse dataset [18], generative tools [36, 53], and artist-designed models [RP].
        *   **Procedure**: Visualization of skeletons, blend weights, and animation results (for cases without ground truth) [RP].
        *   **Fairness Procedure**: Manual setting of bones to similar poses in 3D modeling software for animation rendering when comparing methods with different skeleton topologies [RP].

*   **Evaluation Results and Performance Comparison**:
    *   **Commercial Software Comparison (Meshy, Tripo)**:
        *   **Meshy [36]**:
            *   Tends to enlarge influence regions of skinning weights, leading to incorrect limb deformations [RP].
            *   Fails to extract rest pose for some complex input shapes [RP].
            *   Animates characters in an "unnatural way" [RP].
        *   **Tripo [53]**:
            *   Better blend weights than Meshy, but often produces inaccurate and unmatched bones [RP].
            *   Difficulty determining correct rest state for unusual input poses [RP].
        *   **Both [36, 53]**: Cannot predict finger bones, resulting in coarse hand poses [RP].
        *   **Proposed Method**: Produces complete and accurate bones, suitable blend weights, and natural, fluid animations [RP].
    *   **RigNet Comparison [57]**:
        *   **RigNet**:
            *   Often produces unnecessary topological structures [RP].
            *   Bone prediction errors observed in Tab. 2 [RP].
            *   Weights often fail to maintain spatial smoothness, leading to fragmented animation results [RP].
        *   **Proposed Method**: Demonstrated superiority in comparison cases (Fig. 5) [RP].
    *   **Mixamo [1] and Anything World [33] Comparison**:
        *   **Limitations**: Rely heavily on symmetry or pose simplicity (e.g., T-pose, A-pose) and produce errors with complex inputs [RP].
        *   **Anything World**: Produces significant errors when extracting arm bones for characters with tails [RP].
        *   **Mixamo**: Fails to distinguish left/right sides, producing mirrored skeletons (e.g., ninja case) [RP].
        *   **Both**: Predicted pose-to-rest transformations suffer from unnatural deformations for non-rest input characters [RP].
        *   **Proposed Method**: Maintained good results for challenging cases where commercial software failed (Fig. S7) [RP].
    *   **Other Baselines**:
        *   Additional comparisons with Meshy [36], Tripo [53] (Fig. S4), TADA [32], and HumanGaussian [35] (Fig. S5) further confirm effectiveness, robustness, and generalizability [RP].
    *   **State-of-the-Art Claim**: The proposed method produces complete and accurate bones, suitable blend weights, and natural, fluid animations, outperforming commercial tools and RigNet on various metrics and qualitative assessments [RP]. It also shows robustness to tricky cases and various input poses [RP].

generated_card.Model details:
### Model details

**Model Architecture**
*   **Model Type**: SpinePose is a family of 2D human pose estimation models [HF].
*   **Backbone**: Based on variants of **RTMPose** [RP].
*   **Keypoints**: Predicts a **37-keypoint skeleton** [HF, GH].
    *   Includes **9 keypoints** directly along the spine chain [GH].
    *   Includes standard body joints, spine, pelvis, and feet regions [HF].
    *   Specifically, three keypoints for the **cervical spine**, five keypoints for the **thoracolumbar region**, and one keypoint for the **sacral region** [RP].
    *   Additionally labels left and right **sternoclavicular joints** [RP].
    *   Annotates **six keypoints on the feet** [RP].
*   **Loss Functions**:
    *   **Positional loss** for keypoint-level accuracy [RP].
    *   **Structure loss** (<math display="inline">\mathcal{L}_{\text{structure}}</math>) to enforce anatomical consistency by minimizing bone orientation penalty [RP].
    *   **Spine smoothness loss** (<math display="block">\mathcal{L}_{\text{spine}} = \text{MSE}\left(\{\mathbf{s}_i\}, \{\tilde{\mathbf{s}}_i\}\right).</math>) encourages a smooth predicted spine trajectory [RP].
*   **Training**: Trained with a **teacher-student framework**; teacher outputs heatmaps for `Kbody`, student for `Kstudent` [RP]. Only the student model is used during inference [GH].
*   **Variants**: Four SpinePose variants available:
    *   **Small**: 0.72 GFLOPS [HF].
    *   **Medium**: 1.98 GFLOPS [HF].
    *   **Large**: 4.22 GFLOPS [HF].
    *   **X-Large**: 17.37 GFLOPS [HF].

**Intended uses**
*   **Primary Use Cases**:
    *   Human body and spine joint localization from RGB images or videos [HF].
    *   Real-time motion analysis for research, animation, or sports applications [HF].
    *   Augmentation of general-purpose pose estimators for anatomically rich tasks [HF].
*   **Input**: RGB images or videos [HF].
*   **Output**: 2D heatmaps for 37 keypoints; ONNX checkpoints contain only the student model for inference [GH, RP].
*   **Format**: Annotations follow the **COCO format**, compatible with MMPose, Detectron2, or similar frameworks [GH].

**Additional information**
*   **Developer(s)**: Muhammad Saif Ullah Khan [GH].
*   **Model Version**: v2.0.2 [GH].
    *   Added detector selection: `--detector rfdetr|yolox` [GH].
    *   Integrated RF-DETR as an alternative detector [GH].
*   **Release Date**: No info available.
*   **License**: CC-BY-NC-4.0 License [GH].
    *   Commercial use is prohibited [GH].
    *   Attribution is required for research or educational applications [GH].
*   **References & Citations**:
    *   **Repository**: [MC_4:LINK] [HF].
    *   **Paper**: CVPR Workshops 2025 (CVSPORTS), titled "Towards Unconstrained 2D Pose Estimation of the Human Spine" and "SIMSPINE: A Biomechanics-Aware Simulation Framework for 3D Spine Motion Annotation and Benchmarking" [HF, GH].
    *   **Demo**: [MC_4:LINK] [HF].
    *   **Citation**: If used, please cite the related publications [GH].
*   **Acknowledgements**: No info available.
*   **Disclaimer**: No info available.

generated_card.Training:
### Training

**Dataset:**
*   **Data Collection Process:**
    *   **SpineTrack-Unreal:** Synthetic dataset generated using Unreal Engine 5 with high-fidelity avatars [RP]. Sixteen diverse 3D human models performed ten distinct motion sequences captured from five calibrated camera views [RP]. Rendered images blended with 1,000 real-world indoor and outdoor backgrounds from OpenImagesV7 using the Segment Anything Model (SAM) [RP].
    *   **SpineTrack-Real:** Constructed using an iterative active learning pipeline [RP]. Images selected from multiple sources (e.g., COCO, YogaPose) based on annotation ease [RP]. Pre-trained model (RTMPose-L) detected visible body and feet joints where ground-truth was unavailable [RP]. Shoulder and hip joints used to fit smooth curves around the spinal region for initial spine guesstimates [RP].
    *   **Active Learning:** SpineTrack-Real annotations refined iteratively [RP]. A spine-aware pose model trained with SpineTrack-Unreal established a baseline [RP]. For each batch, the trained model predicted spine keypoints; human annotators corrected low-confidence or erroneous predictions [RP]. Updated labels merged back, and the model was fine-tuned at each cycle [RP]. The synthetic subset was primarily used within the active learning pipeline, but all released SpinePose models were trained exclusively on the real portion of the dataset [RP].
*   **Number of Samples:**
    *   **SpineTrack-Unreal:** Approximately 25k annotated frames [RP]. Nine detailed spinal keypoints with biomechanical alignment through OpenSim [RP].
    *   **SpineTrack-Real:** Over 33k real-world annotations and SpineTrack-format initial pseudo-labels [RP]. Includes nine detailed spinal landmarks in addition to COCO joints [RP].
    *   **Total SpineTrack Dataset:** Images (19.4 GB) and annotations (24.8 MB) [RP].

**Pre-processing:**
*   **Data Preparation:** Images selected from sources like COCO and YogaPose [RP].
*   **Annotations:** 3D annotations from UE5 skeleton system projected into 2D image space for SpineTrack-Unreal [RP]. Ground-truth annotations for SpineTrack-Real were refined via active learning with human correction [RP]. All annotations are in COCO format [RP].
*   **Image Blending:** Rendered images for SpineTrack-Unreal were blended with 1,000 real-world backgrounds from OpenImagesV7 [RP].

**Hyperparameter details:**
*   **Optimizer:** AdamW [RP].
*   **Initial Learning Rate:** <math display="inline">4 \times 10^{-3}</math> [RP].
*   **Learning Rate Schedule:** Short linear warm-up for the first 1000 iterations, then a cosine annealing schedule decaying to 5% of initial value by training end [RP].
*   **Batch Size:** Scaled linearly to maintain an effective batch size of 1024 across experiments [RP]. Set to fit within GPU memory [RP].
*   **Epoch Count:** Fine-tuned for <math display="inline">max\_epochs = 10</math> epochs [RP].
*   **Loss Functions:** A weighted sum of four losses:
    *   **Positional (Keypoint) Loss:** KL-divergence between predicted and ground-truth heatmaps for accurate keypoint localization [RP]. Ensures student learns SpineTrack keypoints from ground-truth annotations [RP].
    *   **Distillation Loss:** KL-divergence between student and teacher predictions for body keypoints ($K_{body}$) [RP]. Aligns student's predictions with teacher's on known body joints to approximate teacher's distribution [RP].
    *   **Structure Loss:** No info available from provided text for its specific formulation beyond being included in the overall objective along with Spine Smoothness loss.
    *   **Spine Smoothness Loss:** Regularizes consecutive vertebral keypoints to form a smooth curve [RP]. Uses a differentiable smoothing operation with sigmoid function and a distance threshold T [RP].
*   **Loss Weights:** Scalar weights <math display="inline">\alpha, \beta, \gamma_1, \gamma_2</math> balance keypoint accuracy, teacher knowledge retention, and anatomically consistent poses [RP].

**Training:**
*   **Training Procedures:**
    *   **Distillation-based Framework:** A student model (S) taught by a body pose expert teacher model (T) to predict an extended skeleton including teacher's body keypoints and SpineTrack spine keypoints [RP]. This retains generalization on smaller datasets [RP].
    *   **Initialization:** Student network initialized with weights from a pre-trained teacher [RP]. Head architecture modified by expanding output layer(s) for new spine keypoints [RP]. Expanded layers' weights copied into original matrix rows, and new rows initialized from a normal distribution based on existing weights [RP].
    *   **Fine-tuning:** Models fine-tuned for 10 epochs on new spine keypoints [RP].
*   **Training Setup:**
    *   **Pre-training:** All SpinePose models start from Body8-pretrained RTMPose weights [RP].
    *   **Resources:** Batch size set to fit within **GPU memory** [RP].
    *   **Input Size:** <math display="inline">256 \times 192</math> for fair comparison [RP].
    *   **Duration:** <math display="inline">max\_epochs = 10</math> epochs [RP].

generated_card.Evaluation:
### Evaluation

*   **Evaluation Setup**:
    *   **Metrics**:
        *   Standard COCO-style **Average Precision (AP)** and **Average Recall (AR)** across varying overlap thresholds [RP].
        *   For SpineTrack, AP/AR are reported for **body**, **feet**, and **spine** subsets, and overall for all 37 keypoints [RP].
        *   Bone **orientation difference** (structure loss) for predicted vs. ground-truth bone angles [RP].
    *   **Test Sets/Held-out Data**:
        *   **SpineTrack dataset** [RP].
        *   Public human pose estimation benchmarks: **COCO**, **Halpe26** [RP].
    *   **Procedures - Quantitative Analysis**:
        *   Ground-truth bounding boxes used for SpineTrack [RP].
        *   Pretrained person detector used for COCO and Halpe26 [RP].
        *   Ablation studies conducted to evaluate impact of each loss component using SpinePose-I model [RP].
        *   RMSE of 2–4 cm for standard body keypoints used as reference from a state-of-the-art pose model [RP].
        *   Any SpineTrack keypoints with RMSE above 10 cm (compared to baseline) were manually refined [RP].
    *   **Procedures - Qualitative Analysis**:
        *   Qualitative discussion based on spine predictions in diverse athletic and fitness scenarios [RP].

*   **Evaluation Results**:
    *   **SpineTrack Annotations**: Achieved reliability comparable to conventional human pose datasets [RP]. Spine keypoints matched or surpassed baseline accuracy (2–4 cm RMSE) [RP].
    *   **SpinePose Models**: Benchmarked against state-of-the-art lightweight pose estimation methods on COCO, Halpe, and SpineTrack datasets [RP].
    *   Results include AP and AR under varying Object Keypoint Similarity (OKS) thresholds [RP].
    *   Specific evaluation scores and error rates are summarized in tables within the research paper [RP].
    *   Evaluation can be reproduced using the evaluation branch of the repository: [PP_18:CODE] [RP].
    *   Halpe evaluations used only 26 body keypoints [RP].

*   **Performance Comparison**:
    *   The model (SpinePose) aims to achieve state-of-the-art results [RP].
    *   SpinePose models are highlighted for comparison against other methods [RP].
    *   Qualitative analysis indicates the model effectively tracks torsos despite occlusions and non-traditional poses [RP].
    *   Spine alignment stability in actions like squatting helps reveal subtle posture deviations [RP].
    *   Accurately locates spine curvature during complex movements (e.g., diving) and dense crowds [RP].
    *   Significant advancement over current state-of-the-art methods that represent the spine with a straight line [RP]. This opens doors for clinical-grade spine pose estimation [RP].

generated_card.Model details:
### Model details

**Model Architecture**:
*   The BiRefNet explicitly decomposes High-Resolution (HR) Dichotomous Image Segmentation (DIS) into two modules: a **Localization Module (LM)** and a **Reconstruction Module (RM)** [RP].
*   It utilizes **bilateral reference** (Inward Reference and Outward Reference) within the RM, leveraging source images at original scales and gradient priors [RP].
*   The LM's encoder output is fed into a classification module using a global average pooling layer and a fully connected layer [RP].
*   The model incorporates **ASPP modules** for multi-context fusion to enlarge receptive fields for large objects and focus on local features for precision [RP].
*   The RM uses **BiRef blocks**, which replace vanilla residual blocks with **Reconstruction Blocks (RBs)** [RP].
*   RBs employ deformable convolutions with hierarchical receptive fields (<math display="inline">1 \times 1</math>, <math display="inline">3 \times 3</math>, <math display="inline">7 \times 7</math>) and an adaptive average pooling layer [RP].
*   Features extracted by different receptive fields are concatenated as <math display="inline">\mathcal{F}_i^{\theta}</math>, followed by a 1x1 convolution and batch normalization to generate RM output <math display="inline">\mathcal{F}_i^{d'}</math> [RP].
*   Coarse maps are predicted by the first BiRef block and then reconstructed into higher-resolution versions by subsequent BiRef blocks [RP].
*   The output feature of each BiRef block <math display="inline">\mathcal{F}_i^d</math> is added with its lateral feature <math display="inline">\mathcal{F}_i^l</math> from the LM at each stage (<math display="inline">\{\mathcal{F}_i^{d+} = \textit{Upsample} \uparrow (\mathcal{F}_i^d + \mathcal{F}_i^l), i = 1\} </math>, 3, 2, 1) [RP].
*   Intermediate predictions <math display="inline">\{\mathcal{M}_i\}_{i=3}^1</math> are generated by multi-stage supervision with ascending resolutions [RP].
*   The final predicted maps <math display="inline">\mathcal{M}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times 1\times H\times W}</math> are obtained by passing the last decoding feature <math display="inline">\mathcal{F}_1^{d+}</math> through an <math display="inline">1\times 1</math> convolution layer [RP].
*   **Loss functions** include:
    *   **IoU loss** for region-aware supervision [RP].
    *   **SSIM loss** (Structural Similarity Index Measure) for boundary-aware supervision, defined as <math display="block">L_{SSIM} = 1 - \frac{(2\mu_x \mu_y + C_1)(2\sigma_{xy} + C_2)}{(\mu_x^2 + \mu_y^2 + C_1)(\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + C_2)}, \quad (4)</math> [RP].
*   **Parameter count**: No info available.
*   **Number of layers**: No info available.

**Intended uses**:
*   The primary use case is **High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation (DIS)** [RP, GH].
*   It is trained on DIS-TR and validated on DIS-TEs and DIS-VD for standard DIS [GH].
*   The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on three tasks: DIS, High-Resolution Saliency Object Detection (HRSOD), and Camouflaged Object Detection (COD) [GH].
*   Potential practical applications include:
    *   **Crack detection** in architectural structures (e.g., walls) [RP].
    *   Integration into tools like **ComfyUI** for foreground segmentation and subsequent stable diffusion model processing [RP].
    *   **Matting foreground segmentation** [RP].
    *   Extracting foreground subjects for **text placement** [GH].

**Additional information**:
*   **Model version(s)**: No info available.
*   **Developer(s)**: No info available.
*   **Release date**: March 7, 2024 [RP].
*   **License type**: No info available.
*   **Reference Paper**: "Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation" [RP, GH].
*   **Conference**: CAAI AIR 2024 [RP, GH].
*   **Citations**: [59] (Architecture health), [28] (COCO dataset), [29, 38] (Previous segmentation works), [3] (ASPP modules), [4] (Deformable convolutions), [33] (PyTorch 2.0) [RP].
*   **Acknowledgements**:
    *   @Freepik for GPU resources for training higher resolution models [GH].
    *   @fal for GPU resources for training general BiRefNet models [GH].
    *   @not-lain for help with HuggingFace deployment [GH].
    *   FAL.AI, Freepik, Redmond.ai, Alibaba-ICBU [GH].
*   **Disclaimer**: This project needs more GPU resources for video tasks and efficient model designs on higher-resolution images [GH]. Weights from some third-party projects (e.g., briaai/RMBG-2.0) are for non-commercial use only and may not be aware of transparency due to DIS task training focusing on binary masks [GH].

generated_card.Training:
### Training

**Dataset**:
*   For **DIS** (Distortion-Invariant Segmentation), **DIS5K-TR** is used as the training set [RP].
*   For **HRSOD** (High-Resolution Salient Object Detection), combinations of **HRSOD**, **UHRSD**, and **DUTS** are used for training [RP].
*   For **COD** (Camouflaged Object Detection), concealed samples from **CAMO-TR** and **COD10K-TR** are used [RP].
*   Training sets can be downloaded from [PP_161:LINK] or BaiduDisk [PP_162:LINK] [RP].
*   **DIS-TR** has **219** categories [RP].

**Pre-processing**:
*   All images are **resized to <math display="inline">1024 \times 1024</math>** for training and testing [RP].
*   **Horizontal flip** is the only data augmentation used [RP].
*   Generated segmentation maps are resized to original GT map size for evaluation using **bilinear interpolation** [RP].

**Hyperparameter details**:
*   **Optimizer**: Adam [22] [RP].
*   **Initial Learning Rate**:
    *   <math display="inline">10^{-4}</math> for DIS batches [RP].
    *   <math display="inline">10^{-5}</math> for HRSOD and COD batches [RP]. No further clarification on "batches" is available.
*   **Batch Size**: **N=4 per GPU** [RP].
*   **Loss Functions**: A weighted combination of four losses [RP]:
    *   **BCE Loss** (Binary Cross-Entropy): Pixel-aware supervision formulated as <math display="block">L_{BCE} = -\sum_{(i,j)} [G(i,j)\log(M(i,j)) + (1 - G(i,j))\log(1 - M(i,j))],</math> [RP].
    *   **IoU Loss** (Intersection over Union) [RP].
    *   **SSIM Loss** (Structural Similarity Index Measure) [RP].
    *   **CE Loss** (Cross-Entropy): Semantic-aware supervision formulated as <math display="block">L_{CE} = -\sum_{c=1}^{N} y_{o,c} \log(p_{o,c}), \tag{5}</math> [RP].
*   **Loss Weights**:
    *   <math display="inline">\lambda_1</math> (BCE), <math display="inline">\lambda_2</math> (IoU), <math display="inline">\lambda_3</math> (SSIM), and <math display="inline">\lambda_4</math> (CE) are initially set to **30, 0.5, 10, and 5** respectively [RP].
    *   These weights aim to keep losses on a similar quantitative level [RP].

**Training**:
*   **Training Procedures**:
    *   The model benefits from **multi-stage supervision (MSS)**, which accelerates convergence and reduces training epochs by half compared to long training [RP].
    *   **Fine-tuning**: The model is fine-tuned with **IoU loss** for the **last 20 epochs** [RP].
    *   **Context feature fusion** and **image pyramid inputs** are used on the backbone for HR image processing [RP].
    *   A masking strategy alleviates non-target noise in gradient information by performing morphological operations on intermediate predictions <math display="inline">\mathcal{M}_i</math> and using dilated <math display="inline">\mathcal{M}_i</math> as a mask [RP].
    *   InRef adaptively crops high-resolution image patches <math display="inline">\{\mathcal P_{k=1}^N\}</math> and stacks them with features <math display="inline">\mathcal F_i^{d+}</math> for the Reference Module (RM) [RP]. This avoids issues found in other methods that either add <math display="inline">\mathcal I</math> only at the last decoding stage or resize <math display="inline">\mathcal I</math> [RP].
    *   OutRef uses gradient labels <math display="inline">G_i^{gt}</math> and predicted gradient maps <math display="inline">\hat{G}_i</math> to generate gradient referring attention <math display="inline">\mathcal{A}_i^G</math>, which is then multiplied by <math display="inline">\mathcal{F}_i^{d'}</math> [RP].
*   **Epoch Count**:
    *   **DIS**: **600 epochs** [RP].
    *   **HRSOD**: **150 epochs** [RP].
    *   **COD**: **150 epochs** [RP].
    *   For DIS5K, the model converges target localization and rough structures in **~200 epochs**, but fine details improve up to **400 epochs** [RP]. MSS helps achieve 400-epoch performance in 200 epochs [RP].
*   **Training Setup**:
    *   Trained using **PyTorch [33]** [RP].
    *   Trained on **eight NVIDIA A100 GPUs** [RP].
    *   A re-trained version was successfully developed on a **single A100-40G GPU** [RP].
    *   Multi-GPU training can be enabled or disabled via a variable in `config.py` [RP].
    *   GPU support from Freepik for huge training (~3 weeks) on H200x4 GPUs for `BiRefNet_HR` (2048x2048 images) [RP].
    *   Fine-tuning from existing weights continues the epoch count indicated in the weight file name [RP].
*   **Duration**: No specific duration in terms of hours/days is provided, but training on H200x4 GPUs took "~3 weeks" for `BiRefNet_HR` [PP_34:LINK].

generated_card.Evaluation:
### Evaluation

*   **Evaluation Setup**:
    *   **Tasks**: High-resolution dichotomous image segmentation (DIS), high-resolution salient object detection (HRSOD), concealed object detection (COD), and salient object detection (SOD) [RP].
    *   **Test Sets**:
        *   **DIS**: DIS5K (DIS-TE1, DIS-TE2, DIS-TE3, DIS-TE4) [RP].
        *   **HRSOD**: DAVIS-S [53], HRSOD-TE [53], UHRSD-TE [46] [RP].
        *   **COD**: CAMO-TE [25], COD10K-TE [9], NC4K [31] [RP].
        *   **Supplementary (Low-resolution SOD)**: DUTS-TE [44], DUT-OMRON [48] [RP].
    *   **Metrics**:
        *   **S-measure** [7] (structural similarity, S_α) [RP]. Evaluates structural similarity between saliency and ground truth (GT) maps [RP].
        *   **F-measure** [1] (weighted harmonic mean of precision and recall) [RP]. Max F-measure (F_β^max) used, with β set to 0.3 [RP].
        *   **E-measure** [8] (enhanced-alignment measure) [RP]. Evaluates local and global similarity between predicted and GT maps [RP]. Both max (E_φ^max) and mean (E_φ^mean) E-measure are used [RP].
        *   **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)**: Pixel-level metric measuring absolute difference between non-binarized predicted results and GT maps [RP].
        *   **Relax HCE** [37] (Human Correction Efforts, H^r_τ): Quantifies human effort to correct predictions, with τ denoting tolerance [RP].relax HCE with τ=0.85 is used [RP].
    *   **Procedure**: Quantitative comparison with state-of-the-art methods [RP]. Qualitative analysis of segmentation maps [RP].

*   **Evaluation Results**:
    *   **BiRefNet** generally outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods across all evaluated metrics in DIS, HRSOD, and COD tasks [RP].
    *   **DIS Task**: BiRefNet surpasses previous methods in both global shape accuracy (structural similarity, S_m) and pixel-level detail precision (MAE) [RP]. It shows strong performance in metrics relevant for practical applications [RP].
    *   **HRSOD Task**: Achieved significantly higher accuracy on both high-resolution and low-resolution SOD benchmarks [RP]. Recorded an average improvement of **2.0% Sm** compared to the previous SOTA [46] [RP].
    *   **COD Task**: Demonstrated much better performance with an average improvement of **5.6% Sm** on three widely used COD benchmarks [RP].
    *   **Qualitative Analysis**:
        *   **DIS**: BiRefNet effectively eliminates distractors, accurately segments target objects, and precisely segments fine details, including slim shapes and curved edges [RP].
        *   **COD**: Shows superior ability in segmenting occluded objects, tiny/slim objects, and multiple concealed objects, accurately reproducing scattered fragments [RP].

*   **Performance Comparison**:
    *   **State-of-the-Art (SOTA)**: BiRefNet achieves leading results across all four tasks (DIS, HRSOD, COD, SOD), outperforming all previous methods [RP].
    *   It exhibits **remarkable generalization ability** to similar high-resolution tasks [RP].
    *   Task-specific models, such as FSP-Net [18], show weaknesses when applied across different HR segmentation tasks, where BiRefNet maintains leading performance [RP].
    *   BiRefNet's performance is particularly strong in metrics that closely align with the requirements of practical applications [RP].
    *   The model can generate high-resolution segmentation results with fine details (e.g., "hair level") without manual guidance [RP].

generated_card.Model details:
### Model details

**Model Architecture**:
*   The MultiBERTs models are **transformer models** pretrained in a self-supervised fashion [RP].
*   Pretraining was performed on raw texts using two objectives:
    *   **Masked Language Modeling (MLM)**: The model predicts randomly masked words (15% of input) [RP]. This allows for a bidirectional representation [RP].
    *   **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)**: The model predicts if two concatenated sentences (one masked) were originally adjacent [RP].
*   The models learn an inner representation of the English language [RP].
*   Input texts are **lowercased** and tokenized using **WordPiece** [RP].
*   The vocabulary size is **30,000** [RP].
*   Model inputs follow the format: `[CLS] sentence A [SEP] sentence B [SEP]` [RP].

**Intended uses**:
*   The primary use is to be **fine-tuned on downstream tasks** [RP].
*   Can be used for masked language modeling or next sentence prediction directly [RP].
*   Primarily aimed at tasks using the whole sentence, such as **sequence classification, token classification, or question answering** [RP].
*   Not intended for text generation; models like GPT2 are suggested for that purpose [RP].
*   Can extract features for training standard classifiers on labeled sentences [RP].

**Additional information**:
*   **Model Version**: MultiBERTs Seed 4 Checkpoint 60k (uncased) [RP]. This is an intermediate checkpoint; the final checkpoint is `multiberts-seed-4` [RP].
*   **Developer(s)**: Google Research Language team [RP]. The model card itself was written by gchhablani [RP].

generated_card.Evaluation:
### Evaluation

*   **Evaluation Setup**:
    *   **Metrics**:
        *   Performance metric denoted as L(s), an expected performance metric on a test distribution D [RP].
        *   Accuracy [RP].
        *   Log likelihood [RP].
        *   Conjectured generalization to AUC, BLEU score, and expected calibration error [RP].
        *   Bias correlation (r values) [RP].
    *   **Test Sets**:
        *   GLUE development sets [RP].
        *   SQuAD development sets [RP].
        *   Finite evaluation sample of 60 occupations [RP].
    *   **Procedures**:
        *   **Multi-Bootstrap**: A non-parametric bootstrapping procedure estimating estimation error distribution over seeds and test instances [RP].
            *   Supports both paired and unpaired study designs [RP].
            *   Estimates uncertainty due to finite evaluation set and finite number of seeds [RP].
            *   Allows computation of confidence intervals and hypothesis testing [RP].
        *   **Experimental Designs**:
            *   **Design 1: Comparison to a Fixed Baseline**: Compares MultiBERTs to a single external baseline (e.g., human performance, another model like RoBERTa), assessing uncertainty from MultiBERTs' random seeds and test examples [RP].
            *   **Design 2: Paired Samples**: Assesses the effect of an intervention on model behavior, where baseline and intervention models are based on the same pre-trained checkpoint and seed [RP]. Estimates errors for both models and their correlation [RP].
            *   **Design 3: Unpaired Samples**: Compares models where intervention and baseline cannot be built from the same checkpoints or have separate initialization schemes [RP]. Estimates errors but assumes zero correlation between errors [RP].
        *   **Hypothesis Testing**: Recommends testing if a difference is meaningfully different from a predefined threshold (e.g., 0) using Multi-Bootstrap [RP].
            *   A valid p-value is the fraction of bootstrap samples where the estimate $\widehat{\delta}_{boot}$ is negative [RP].

*   **Evaluation Results**:
    *   **MultiBERTs vs. Original BERT** [RP]:
        *   Performance on most GLUE tasks for original BERT falls within the range of MultiBERTs scores [RP].
        *   Original BERT **outperforms all MultiBERTs** on **QQP** [RP].
        *   Original BERT **under-performs all MultiBERTs** on **SQuAD** [RP].
        *   Performance discrepancies attributed to randomness and training setup differences [RP].
        *   MultiBERTs models (2M steps) outperform original BERT on SQuAD 2.0 with a **95% confidence range of 1.9% to 2.9%** and **p < 0.001** [RP].
    *   **Impact of CDA-incr Intervention** [RP]:
        *   Overall accuracy is consistent across pre-training seeds: **62.6% ± 1.2%** for the base model [RP].
        *   CDA-incr intervention resulted in only a small, not statistically significant change in overall accuracy [RP].
        *   Significant variation in bias correlation: r values between **0.1 and 0.7** depending on pretraining seed [RP].
        *   CDA-incr's r values range between **0.0 and 0.4** [RP].
        *   Paired Multi-Bootstrap showed CDA-incr significantly reduces bias by **$\delta_b = -0.12$ with p = 0.001** [RP].
    *   **Ablation Study (Sampling Dimensions)** [RP]:
        *   Sampling only over seeds (fixed examples) yields **p = 0.005** [RP].
        *   Sampling only over examples (fixed seeds) yields **p = 0.053** [RP].
        *   Failing to account for finite samples along either dimension can lead to overconfident conclusions [RP].
    *   **Winogender Metric for Coreference Models** [RP]:
        *   Unpaired Multi-Bootstrap analysis (seed 0 as base, seed 1 as experiment) indicates a significant effect of **$\delta = 0.203$ (p = 0.009)** [RP]. This confirms that seed 1 has different properties compared to other seeds (0, 2, 3, 4) [RP].

*   **Performance Comparison and State-of-the-Art**:
    *   MultiBERTs performance on GLUE is generally comparable to the original BERT across most tasks [RP].
    *   On QQP, original BERT outperforms MultiBERTs [RP].
    *   On SQuAD, MultiBERTs outperform original BERT [RP].
    *   The paper provides a framework for robust evaluation rather than claiming a specific model achieves state-of-the-art results [RP]. It focuses on quantifying uncertainty in comparisons [RP].

**Model Architecture**:
*   The model is a fine-tuned version of `microsoft/layoutxlm-base` [HF].
*   It is described as a **Document Understanding model** finetuned at the line level on the DocLayNet base dataset [HF].
*   No information available on parameter count or number of layers for the fine-tuned model.
*   **Input**: The model processes documents. Inference is performed at the line level [HF].
*   **Output**: At inference time, the model calculates best probabilities to assign a label to each line bounding box [HF].

**Intended uses**:
*   The primary goal is to obtain **high-quality ML models** capable of accurate document-layout analysis on a wide variety of challenging layouts [RP].
*   Specifically, the model is intended for **document layout segmentation and analysis** [HF].

**Additional information**:
*   **Model Version**: No specific version number is provided for the fine-tuned model. The DocLayNet dataset was made available on Hugging Face on January 26th, 2023 [HF].

generated_card.Training:
### Training

**Dataset:**
*   **Data Collection Process:**
    *   Four phases: data source identification, label definition, annotator training, and production annotation [RP].
    *   Sourced PDF documents from publication repositories (e.g., arXiv), government offices, company websites, and financial report/patent directories [RP].
    *   Excluded scanned documents to ensure rectangular bounding-box annotation compatibility [RP].
    *   Uploaded and parsed PDFs into Corpus Conversion Service (CCS) [RP].
    *   Subsampled pages to achieve a desired balance across document categories [RP].
    *   Prioritized title pages and pages with figures or tables using pre-trained object detection models (PubLayNet) [RP].
    *   974 pages were reference-annotated by a core team member for training material [RP].
    *   32 trained annotators performed production annotation [RP].
*   **Number of Samples:**
    *   80,000 pages were annotated for the dataset [RP].

**Pre-processing:**
*   **Data Preparation:**
    *   PDF documents were uploaded and parsed in the Corpus Conversion Service (CCS) [RP].
    *   Subsampling of pages was performed to balance document categories [RP].
    *   Leveraged pre-trained object detection models from PubLayNet for page selection [RP].
*   **Preprocessing Methods:**
    *   No info available for data filtering or augmentation methods explicitly stated during initial data preparation. (Note: Data augmentation was suggested as a future improvement [RP]).

**Hyperparameter details:**
*   **Learning Rate:** `2e-05` [RP].
*   **Train Batch Size:** `8` [RP].
*   **Eval Batch Size:** `16` [RP].
*   **Seed:** `42` [RP].
*   **Optimizer:** Adam with `betas=(0.9, 0.999)` and `epsilon=1e-08` [RP].
*   **LR Scheduler Type:** `linear` [RP].
*   **LR Scheduler Warmup Ratio:** `0.1` [RP].
*   **Number of Epochs:** `3` [RP].
*   **Mixed Precision Training:** Native AMP [RP].

**Training:**
*   **Training Procedures:**
    *   The model was **fine-tuned** at the line level [RP].
    *   Used chunks of 384 tokens with an overlap of 128 tokens [RP].
    *   Trained with all layout and text data from all dataset pages [RP].
    *   **Annotation Training:**
        *   Annotation staff were trained using a subset of pages with two complexity levels [RP].
        *   Required passing two exam levels with high annotation quality before production work [RP].
        *   Practice iterations lasted 12 weeks, with 8 out of 40 annotators not passing [RP].
*   **Training Setup:**
    *   **Resources:** 32 annotators were assembled for production annotation [RP].
    *   **Duration:** Production annotation phase took approximately three months [RP].
    *   **Hardware:** No info available on specific GPU or TPU usage; implied to be software-based annotation for data creation [RP].

generated_card.Evaluation:
### Evaluation

*   **Evaluation Setup**:
    *   **Metrics**: Mean Average Precision (mAP) with 10 overlap thresholds ranging from 0.5 to 0.95 (mAP@0.5-0.95) [RP]. Scores are computed using the COCO API evaluation code [RP].
    *   **Test Set**: DocLayNet's predefined test set [RP].
        *   Split on full-document boundaries to prevent pages from the same document appearing in different sets, avoiding inflated accuracy estimates [RP].
        *   Ensures less frequent labels are equally represented in train and test sets [RP].
    *   **Procedures**:
        *   Both training and evaluation used RGB images with dimensions of **1025x1025 pixels** [RP].
        *   For training, a single annotation was used for redundantly annotated pages [RP].
    *   **Qualitative Analysis**: Visual inspection of layout predictions on test-set pages, noting potential issues like overlapping boxes or missing elements [RP].

*   **Evaluation Results**:
    *   **Baseline Models (mAP)** [RP]:
        *   **Mask R-CNN**: No info available.
        *   **Faster R-CNN**: No info available.
        *   **YOLOv5**: No info available.
    *   Overall mAP for baseline models was between **6% and 10% lower** than mAP computed from pairwise human annotations on triple-annotated pages [RP].
    *   This gap indicates the dataset's challenge for ML approaches [RP].

*   **Performance Comparison**:
    *   **Mask R-CNN** and **Faster R-CNN** produced very comparable mAP scores [RP].
        *   Suggests pixel-based image segmentation from bounding boxes did not significantly improve predictions for these models [RP].
    *   **YOLOv5x** outperformed humans on selected labels such as **Text**, **Table**, and **Picture** [RP].
        *   This is attributed to these categories being abundant and visually distinctive [RP].
    *   The variation in mAP between baseline models was relatively low [RP].
    *   Human annotation provides an estimated "natural" upper bound on segmentation accuracy [RP].
        *   This baseline consistency evaluation helps define target accuracy and prevent overfitting in deep neural networks [RP].
